lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The call comes at eight-fourteen. Bay two. Sixty-three, male, chest pain that had stopped being chest pain before he was off the trolley. Maeve hears it from the triage desk and moves before the announcement finishes.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Bay two is already arranged by the time she gets there. Reza at the head, airway kit laid out. Theo on compressions, his sleeves pushed back, counting under his breath. The crash cart is unlocked. The defibrillator is charging. This is the choreography — not thought, just motion, the ER absorbing what comes in the way it always absorbs it.

lines.speaker: REZA
lines.text: No output. Coarse VF. Theo, keep going. Maeve, drugs.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She takes the tray. Adrenaline first, then amiodarone, sequence clear in her head, same sequence she has used a hundred times. She draws up the first syringe with her right hand and her left hand reaches for the second vial and stops.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The syringe she drew up first is not adrenaline.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She had prepared it twenty minutes earlier for a different patient in bay four — a sedation dose, sitting in the labelled tray on the right side of the cart, nowhere near where this syringe should be. And somewhere between the triage desk and this room it had moved, or she had moved it, or she is not certain which, and none of that matters now because it is in her hand.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She looks at it. One second. Less than one second.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She sets it down on the far edge of the tray, flap-side down, out of reach. Picks up the correct vial. Draws adrenaline. Checks the label. Checks it again. Does all of this with the quiet deliberate pace of someone who has always been thorough, so that thorough is all anyone sees.

lines.speaker: REZA
lines.text: Push adrenaline.

lines.speaker: MAEVE
lines.text: Pushing now.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Her voice is level. It is the only thing that matters and it is level. She watches the monitor. Theo counts. The room makes its sounds — the ventilator, the compression rhythm, the quiet single-syllable language of people who do not need to explain themselves to each other.

lines.speaker: THEO
lines.text: Hold compressions. Rhythm check.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Everyone steps back. The monitor traces something slow and irregular — not organized, not enough — and Reza reads it the same moment everyone else does.

lines.speaker: REZA
lines.text: Resume. Two more minutes. Second adrenaline on standby.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Outside the bay, through the narrow window in the curtain, the ward clerk from admin passes with a folder — the HR paperwork that came through the station desk earlier, Theo's filing, the cover sheet visible to anyone standing at the nurses' station this morning. The clerk does not look in. The room has no room for anything outside itself. No one notices.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Maeve draws the second dose. She is careful in a way that reads as her ordinary careful — thorough, slightly slower than fast, nothing that draws an eye. Her hands are doing what they are supposed to do. She does not look at the syringe she set on the edge of the tray. It is still there.

lines.speaker: THEO
lines.text: Output. Is that — that's sinus.

lines.speaker: REZA
lines.text: Confirmed. Hold compressions. Good. Maeve, post-ROSC protocol.

lines.speaker: MAEVE
lines.text: Already on it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She is. She prepares the infusions in the correct order. She draws labels in her own handwriting. She does not make a sound and she does not shake and when Reza looks at her across the bed — that particular look he has, not a question, not quite — she meets it with the flat attention of someone whose face has nothing to report.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The patient is moved to CCU at eight-forty-one. The bay is cleaned. Theo bags the sharps. Maeve writes the drug record at the nurses' station, precise, every dose logged in the correct sequence, because every dose was given correctly.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She does not write the syringe she set on the far edge of the tray. Nothing happened. There is no entry for a thing that did not happen.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Her hands are steady now. They were not steady earlier. Only she knows by how much.
